timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ministry of Amos

sections.header: Verses 1-9 — A Lament for Fallen Israel

heb.paragraph: Amos pronounces a funeral dirge over Israel while the nation is still alive. The qinah meter (3+2 stress pattern: three beats followed by two) creates an audible limp in the verse, imitating the halting rhythm of a mourner. The pronouncement of death before death is the ultimate prophetic provocation.

The imperative 'seek the LORD and live' (v. 6) is the book's central positive command. The verb darash means to inquire, investigate, pursue — not passive waiting but active pursuit of God. It stands in contrast to the empty ritual of 'seeking Bethel' (v. 5), which produces death rather than life.

panels.ctx: Chapter 5 opens the third 'Hear this word' speech and is the theological center of the book. It begins with a funeral lament for Israel (vv. 1-3), pivots to the central command 'Seek the LORD and live' (vv. 4-6), and includes the second doxology (vv. 8-9) as a reminder of who this God is. The passage sets up the fundamental choice: seek YHWH (life) or seek Bethel/Gilgal/Beersheba (death). The decimation statistics (v. 3: a city that sends a thousand will have only a hundred left) make the stakes concrete.

cross.note: 'I have set before you life and death, blessings and curses. Now choose life' — the Mosaic framework Amos invokes with 'seek the LORD and live.'
'Seek the LORD until he comes and showers his righteousness on you' — Hosea's parallel command, sharing Amos' vocabulary.

notes.note: Amos sings a funeral dirge over Israel while the nation still prospers. Prophetic perspective sees the end from the beginning. The 'Fallen Virgin Israel' language is heartbreaking: the nation that should have been protected lies abandoned.

'Seek the LORD and live' is the positive heart of Amos' message. Judgment is not inevitable if repentance is genuine. But seeking God must be distinguished from seeking shrines: Bethel, Gilgal, and Beersheba cannot save.

The second doxology identifies YHWH as the maker of the Pleiades and Orion, the one who turns darkness into dawn. This cosmic God has the power to 'destroy the stronghold' — Samaria's fortresses are nothing to the one who moves stars.

Calvin distinguishes between true seeking (turning to God in repentance and obedience) and false seeking (multiplying religious observance at corrupt shrines). The externals of worship are useless when the heart remains turned from God.

The qinah or funeral dirge meter (3+2 accentual pattern) is used here, the same meter used in the book of Lamentations. The rhythmic 'limp' is audible: the verse trails off, imitating the fading of life.
The Pleiades (kimah) and Orion (kesil) are the two most prominent star clusters/constellations in the ancient Near Eastern sky. Their inclusion in the doxology emphasizes YHWH's cosmic scope.

Stuart identifies the funeral dirge as one of Amos' most provocative rhetorical moves: pronouncing a nation dead while it is at the height of prosperity. The decimation statistics (90% casualties) indicate total military disaster, consistent with the Assyrian conquest that would come within a generation.

Stuart reads 'seek the LORD and live' as the structural center of the entire book. It is the one clear call to repentance in a book dominated by judgment. The contrast between seeking YHWH (life) and seeking Bethel (death) is absolute: there is no middle ground between the living God and dead shrines.

Andersen and Freedman argue that the qinah dirge (vv. 1-3), the call to seek YHWH (vv. 4-6), and the doxology (vv. 8-9) form a triptych: death, life, and divine power. The audience is confronted with the reality of death, offered an alternative, and reminded of the power of the God who makes the offer.

heb.paragraph: Together with tsedaqah ('righteousness'), mishpat forms the most important word pair in prophetic ethics. Justice (mishpat) is the proper functioning of the legal system; righteousness (tsedaqah) is the right ordering of social relationships. Amos demands both, and both are absent.

panels.ctx: This section targets the judicial system directly. The 'gate' was the place of legal proceedings in Israelite cities, where elders heard cases and rendered verdicts. Amos accuses the powerful of hating anyone who speaks honestly at the gate, trampling the poor with excessive grain taxes, and building stone mansions they will never enjoy. The 'perhaps' of v. 15 ('Perhaps the LORD God Almighty will have mercy on the remnant of Joseph') is striking: even the prophet cannot guarantee mercy. It depends on genuine justice.

cross.note: 'He looked for justice (mishpat) but saw bloodshed (mispah), for righteousness (tsedaqah) but heard cries of distress (tseʿaqah)' — Isaiah's parallel indictment using identical vocabulary and devastating wordplay.
Micah's parallel condemnation of leaders who should 'know justice' but instead devour the people.

notes.note: Those who hate the honest advocate at the gate and trample the poor with excessive taxes will never enjoy the stone houses they build or drink the wine from their vineyards. Amos turns the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 against the elite.
'Seek good, not evil, that you may live.' The call to justice is not abstract ethics but concrete reform: establish justice at the gate, protect the vulnerable, hate evil and love good.

Calvin connects Amos' call to seek good with the Reformed emphasis on sanctification: genuine faith produces ethical transformation. The 'perhaps' of v. 15 reflects not divine uncertainty but human contingency — mercy depends on genuine repentance.

The 'taxes of grain' (masʾet bar) imposed on the poor likely refer to illegal or excessive levies. The stone houses built with this extorted wealth will never be enjoyed: the futility curse echoes Deut 28:30, 39.
The phrase 'remnant of Joseph' uses the tribal name Joseph (= Ephraim + Manasseh) for the northern kingdom, perhaps evoking the patriarch's story of survival.

Stuart reads vv. 10-13 as a description of systemic corruption: the courts have been captured by the wealthy, honest testimony is silenced, and the poor are economically crushed. The prudent person keeps quiet (v. 13) not from wisdom but from terror: speaking truth is dangerous in a corrupt system.

Stuart emphasizes that the 'perhaps' (ulay) of v. 15 is theologically significant: it maintains divine freedom. Even genuine repentance does not obligate God — it merely opens the possibility of mercy. This is the most honest thing a prophet can say: repent, and hope.

Andersen and Freedman identify a chiastic structure: vv. 10-12 (accusation) frame v. 13 (the prudent silence), which is then answered by vv. 14-15 (exhortation) and vv. 16-17 (wailing). The chiasm places the silence of the wise at the center, emphasizing how thoroughly corruption has intimidated truth-telling.

sections.header: Verses 18-27 — The Day of the LORD

heb.paragraph: Amos provides the earliest datable use of this concept. Popular expectation saw the Day of the LORD as a day of Israel's triumph over enemies. Amos reverses the concept entirely: it will be darkness, not light — judgment on Israel, not salvation.

panels.ctx: This section contains two of Amos' most famous passages. First, the reversal of the Day of the LORD concept (vv. 18-20): Israel expected a day of triumph, but Amos declares it will be darkness, like escaping a lion only to meet a bear. Second, the rejection of worship without justice (vv. 21-24), climaxing in the iconic verse: 'Let justice roll on like a river, righteousness like a never-failing stream.' The chapter concludes with a historical question (vv. 25-27) suggesting that even in the wilderness, Israel's worship was compromised.

cross.note: 'The day of the LORD is coming — a day of darkness and gloom' — Joel's development of the Day of the LORD tradition that Amos inaugurated.
Isaiah's parallel rejection of worship without justice: 'Stop bringing meaningless offerings! ... Learn to do right; seek justice.'
Jesus' judgment scene, where the criterion is treatment of 'the least of these' — the ethical principle Amos championed.

notes.note: Israel looked forward to the Day of the LORD as a day of national victory. Amos inverts the concept: it will be judgment, not salvation — darkness, not light. The man who escapes a lion meets a bear; the one who reaches home is bitten by a snake. There is no escape.

God hates, despises, and rejects Israel's religious festivals, assemblies, offerings, and songs. The problem is not the rituals themselves but the absence of justice behind them. 'Let justice roll on like a river' is the most famous verse in Amos and one of the most powerful ethical demands in all of Scripture.

The rhetorical question about wilderness worship suggests that even then, Israel's devotion was mixed with idolatry. The coming exile beyond Damascus will complete the cycle: from wilderness wandering to exile.

Calvin warns against presumptuous eschatology: those who eagerly await God's intervention should consider whether they are prepared for his judgment. The Day of the LORD is terrifying for the complacent.

Calvin regards this passage as one of the clearest statements of the prophetic principle that God values ethical obedience over ritual performance. This does not abolish worship but demands that worship be accompanied by justice.

This is the earliest datable reference to the 'Day of the LORD' (yom YHWH). The concept apparently existed in popular theology as a day of Israel's vindication against enemies. Amos radically redefines it as a day of judgment against Israel.

The Hebrew yiggal ('roll on') uses a jussive form expressing a command: justice must roll like a perennial stream (nahal etan), not a seasonal wadi that dries up in summer. The metaphor demands consistent, reliable justice.

Stuart argues that the Day of the LORD concept predates Amos and was rooted in the holy war tradition: YHWH fights for Israel against enemies. Amos' innovation is applying this concept against Israel itself. The successive escape scenarios (lion → bear → snake) create a narrative of inescapable judgment.

Stuart reads vv. 21-24 as the ethical climax of the book. The five verbs of rejection (hate, despise, will not accept, will not regard, will not listen) are followed by one positive command: let justice roll. The image of justice as a perennial stream (not a seasonal wadi) demands reliability and permanence.

Stuart interprets the rhetorical question as implying that wilderness worship, though simpler, was not the pure devotion later tradition assumed. The reference to Sikkuth and Kaiwan (v. 26) identifies specific Mesopotamian astral deities, suggesting syncretism even in the Mosaic period.

Andersen and Freedman read the entire section as a three-part argument: (1) The Day of the LORD will be darkness (vv. 18-20); (2) worship without justice is rejected (vv. 21-24); (3) idolatry has been a chronic problem since the wilderness (vv. 25-27). The three parts correspond to three failures: eschatological presumption, ethical hypocrisy, and religious syncretism.

themes.note: Justice at 10: 'let justice roll on like a river' is the book's iconic demand. Judgment at 9: the Day of the LORD reversal is devastating. Worship at 8: the rejection of worship without justice is central.

rows.text: Funeral Dirge and 'Seek the LORD and Live'

rows.label: vv.10-17
rows.text: Justice at the Gate: Perhaps the LORD Will Have Mercy

rows.label: vv.18-27
rows.text: Day of the LORD and Justice Like a River

lit.note: Chapter 5 is the theological center of the book, structured around three movements: lament (vv. 1-3), call to seek God (vv. 4-9), indictment of injustice (vv. 10-17), and the Day of the LORD reversal (vv. 18-27). The central command 'Seek the LORD and live' is flanked by judgment on every side.

ppl.name: Amos
ppl.text: Amos sings a funeral dirge over Israel while the nation still prospers…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Seek the LORD and Live

translations.text: This is what the Sovereign Lord says to Israel: “Your city that marches out a thousand strong will have only a hundred left; your town that marches out a hundred strong will have only ten left.”
For thus says the Lord GOD: “The city that went out a thousand shall have a hundred left, and that which went out a hundred shall have ten left to the house of Israel.”
Seek the Lord and live, or he will sweep through the tribes of Joseph like a fire; it will devour them, and Bethel will have no one to quench it.
Seek the LORD and live, lest he break out like fire in the house of Joseph, and it devour, with none to quench it for Bethel,
